title: Aging Record Statistics in Saturating Self-Interacting Random Walks
authors: J. Br√©mont, R. Voituriez, O. B√©nichou
abstract: The record age tau_k, defined as the time between the k-th and k+1-st record-breaking events, is a central observable of extreme-value statistics. In Markovian processes, the absence of memory makes tau_k independent of k. How memory breaks this invariance and induces aging, meaning a dependence of tau_k on k, remains a fundamental question, closely connected to widely observed aging phenomena in non-Markovian dynamics. In this Letter, we derive the exact asymptotic distribution of tau_k for saturating self-interacting random walks, a broad class of non-Markovian processes. We uncover two asymptotic regimes, in agreement with recent scaling predictions: at short times (tau much smaller than k squared), record statistics are governed by the geometry of the explored region, while at long times (tau much larger than k squared), memory effects become subdominant and reduce to nontrivial prefactor corrections. Our exact result provides a rare analytic window beyond scaling theory and extends to a framework that fully quantifies aging dynamics in the presence of saturating self-interaction.
summary: This paper provides an exact analytical solution for the record-age statistics in a class of non-Markovian random walks. It demonstrates how memory effects localized at the boundaries of a visited region lead to the emergence of aging phenomena. The work bridges the gap between scaling theory and microscopic dynamics by providing a complete description of the crossover between different asymptotic regimes.
main_problem: Understanding how memory in non-Markovian processes breaks the translation invariance of record ages, leading to aging phenomena where the distribution of time intervals between records depends on the record index.
main_result: The authors derive the exact asymptotic distribution of the record age for saturating self-interacting random walks, uncovering two distinct regimes: a short-time regime governed by the geometry of the explored region and a long-time regime where memory effects act as subdominant prefactor corrections.
method: The study employs the Ray-Knight theorem and its aged extension to map the dynamics to a sum of independent squared Bessel processes (BESQ), using Laplace transforms, path integrals, and hypergeometric functions to obtain exact analytical solutions.
model_or_system: Saturating Self-Interacting Random Walks (SIRWs), specifically the SATW model, where the walker's future motion depends on its history, but memory effects are localized at the boundaries of the visited territory.
key_observables: Record age (tau_k), aged First-Passage Times (FPTs), and the scaling function psi(x).
important_parameters: Parameters alpha and beta (interaction weights), the record index k, and the scaling variable x = k^(-dw) * tau.
main_assumptions: The results rely on the 'saturation' property of the self-interaction, which ensures the process is Markovian in the bulk; the derivation also assumes a continuum scaling limit for large visited territories.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a SATW trajectory showing the visited region and how transition probabilities change at the boundaries; Figure 3 shows the decomposition of the total area into three independent BESQ processes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of aging in record statistics, introduces the SATW model, develops a mathematical framework using the Ray-Knight representation, derives the exact scaling function through Laplace-space analysis, and concludes by discussing asymptotic regimes and universality.
why_interesting: The derivation of exact scaling functions for non-Markovian processes with long-range memory is highly relevant to understanding the dynamics of open quantum systems and many-body systems where environmental memory effects induce non-equilibrium aging.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 1: Trajectory and record ages ѕДk of a SATW. The growing region visited by the walker is represented in blue. Steps taken starting from a local maximum (resp. minimum) are represented by purple (resp. green) arrows, while black steps in the bulk of the visited region have probability 1/2, as the walker behaves as a sim- ple RW in this region. Self-interaction is present at the boundaries, where each upward (resp. downward) purple arrow has probability 1/(1 + ќ±) (resp. ќ±/(1 + ќ±)), while each upward (resp. downward) green arrow has probability ќ≤/(1 + ќ≤) (resp. 1/(1 + ќ≤)).
